questions.question: Explain the concept of an Access Control List (ACL) in operating system protection. How does it differ from a Capability List?
keyPoints: ACL associates permissions with each FILE/OBJECT
Lists subjects (users) and their permitted operations
Capability List associates permissions with each USER/SUBJECT
ACL is file-centric; Capability is user-centric
ACL easier to see who accesses a file; Capability easier to see what a user can access

questions.question: Describe the three main approaches to file protection: Complete, Limited, and Password protection.
keyPoints: Complete Protection: owner specifies exactly file
Limited Protection: owner specifies TYPES of operations who can access the allowed (read/write/execute)
Password Protection: each file has a password; access requires correct password
Complete is most restrictive; Password is simplest but less flexible

questions.question: What are Unix file permissions? Explain the significance of owner, group, and other permissions with examples.
keyPoints: Three categories: owner (user), group, others
Each has Read(4), Write(2), Execute(1) permissions
Example: 755 means rwxr-xr-x (owner full, others read+execute)
Example: 644 means rw-r--r-- (owner read/write, others read only)
Used for discretionary access control (DAC)

questions.question: Define protection domain in operating systems. How does domain switching occur?
keyPoints: Domain is a set of objects and operations permitted on them
Defines what resources a process/user can access
Domain switching allows change of privileges during execution
Example: user process switching to kernel mode for privileged operations
Helps implement principle of least privilege

questions.question: What is the principle of least privilege? Why is it important in operating system protection?
keyPoints: Users/processes should have only minimum access needed for their task
Reduces potential damage from errors or malicious actions
Helps contain security breaches
Commonly implemented through role-based access control
Used in Unix by avoiding root/admin use for daily tasks

questions.question: Explain the access matrix model of protection with the help of a diagram.
keyPoints: Rows represent SUBJECTS (users/processes)
Columns represent OBJECTS (files, devices)
Each cell contains the operations the subject can perform on that object
Abstract model; can be implemented as ACL or Capability Lists
Simple but powerful conceptual framework for protection

questions.question: Differentiate between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC).
keyPoints: DAC: owner discretionarily grants/revokes access (Unix permissions)
MAC: system enforces access based on fixed security labels
DAC is flexible; MAC is rigid and system-controlled
MAC used in military/government (classification levels)
DAC common in general-purpose operating systems

questions.question: Explain in detail the various methods for implementing file protection in operating systems. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each approach.
keyPoints: Approaches: Complete, Limited, Password Protection
ACL (Access Control List) - file-centric, easy to see who accesses
Capability Lists - user-centric, efficient for user perspective
Unix permissions (rwx for owner/group/others)
Encryption for data protection
Disadvantages: ACL can be large; Capabilities need secure storage; Password can be forgotten
Discuss revocation mechanisms for each

questions.question: Describe the Unix/Linux file permission system in detail. How would you: (a) Give read and write permission to owner only? (b) Give full permission to owner, read to group? (c) Explain the purpose of umask and give an example.
keyPoints: Permission bits: Read(4), Write(2), Execute(1)
Three categories: owner, group, others
(a) chmod 600 or chmod u=rw filename
(b) chmod 740 or chmod u=rwx,g=r filename
umask sets default permissions by masking bits
Default file creation: 666 - umask
Default directory creation: 777 - umask
Example: umask 022 gives 755 for directories, 644 for files

questions.question: What is revocation in access control? Explain different methods of revocation with examples.
keyPoints: Revocation: cancelling or modifying previously granted access rights
Importance: when users change roles, leave organization, or need reduced access
Methods: (1) Reversion - rights return to system/default
(2) Transfer - rights moved to another subject
(3) Expiration - rights automatically expire after time
ACL revocation: remove from file's access list
Capability revocation: more complex; need to track and invalidate capabilities
Example: employee leaving company - revoke all system access

questions.question: Discuss the concept of protection domains and how they are implemented in operating systems. How does domain switching enhance system security?
keyPoints: Protection domain defines what resources a process can access
Domain = set of (object, rights) pairs
Each user/process operates within a protection domain
Domain switching allows dynamic change of privileges
Example: user process vs kernel mode in Unix
System call causes switch from user domain to kernel domain
Restricted system calls only available in certain domains
Enforces least privilege - only necessary privileges used
Prevents malicious/wrong programs from accessing unauthorized resources
